rationales.self_direction: He is seriously considering switching labs again because the current path is just 'rinse and repeat for the next three years,' showing a pull toward choosing his own direction.
rationales.stimulation: He is energized by 'VR and neural mapping,' fieldwork in 'COSTA RICA,' and the idea of being in a rainforest instead of routine terminal work.
rationales.security: He is tempted to 'uproot everything again' for a project he admits he doesn't 'even fully know,' favoring uncertainty over stability.
rationales.conformity: He pushes against committee expectations, reacting to 'what will your committee think?' with irritation and wanting something far less conventional.

rationales.stimulation: He keeps getting pulled toward 'flights to New Zealand,' 'pottery classes,' and learning to surf, showing strong attraction to novelty over routine.
rationales.achievement: He admits he had 'nothing to say' about his thesis timeline and cannot stay on task long enough to advance his work.
rationales.security: He feels unable to provide a stable academic plan and worries he 'might not actually finish' the PhD, reflecting conflict with predictability and assurance.
rationales.conformity: He hides both the credit card issue and the thought 'Can I take a gap year?' because he knows these would violate family and program expectations.

rationales.self_direction: He keeps the Singapore message alive as 'a little door that's cracked open' instead of obeying what 'everyone would say' he should do.
rationales.stimulation: The draw is novelty and speed: 'I've never been to Singapore' and he is captivated by the company's 'velocity' versus slow academia.
rationales.security: He contemplates leaving a PhD for a 'random job opportunity,' which would trade a more defined path for a risky unknown.
rationales.conformity: He explicitly frames the interview option as something his parents, Sharma, and 'everyone would say' he should not pursue.
rationales.universalism: What kept him watching was that the company is 'actually helping people' and reaching 'real patients' now, showing concern beyond his immediate circle.

rationales.self_direction: He goes through with the interview and is drawn to a role where someone says he can 'think sideways,' affirming a less conventional path that fits him better.
rationales.stimulation: He is pulled toward the startup because it feels like 'the next shiny thing' and a new direction beyond his current program.
rationales.security: He may leave the lab he transferred into only six months ago for a startup job, which would destabilize his current academic path.
rationales.conformity: He keeps the process secret because once others know, he will have to 'defend it' and face the judgment that he is 'not serious.'

rationales.self_direction: Scheduling the second interview shows he is continuing to pursue a path outside the PhD rather than simply staying put.
rationales.stimulation: The entry centers on the charged excitement of the 'Second interview scheduled' and the pull of a major change.
rationales.achievement: He says he 'definitely bombed' the committee meeting because he 'couldn't focus,' which directly undermined competent performance.

rationales.stimulation: The offer is for relocation to Singapore and building a pipeline 'from scratch,' a highly novel path he is actively weighing.
rationales.achievement: He completed a demanding process, including a four-hour interview and technical assessment, and received an offer for a major role.
rationales.security: He is considering 'blowing up my life again' despite 'no savings' and the possibility that leaving means he 'won't have a PhD.'
rationales.conformity: He is moving toward a choice that conflicts with what his academic program and family expect, and he lies about both research and family matters to avoid that pressure.
rationales.benevolence: He tells Amma that research is 'going great' when it is not, and he frames himself as 'a terrible son' for withholding the truth.

rationales.self_direction: He argues that 'the only way to get facts is to run the experiment,' defending an exploratory choice based on his own judgment.
rationales.stimulation: He is willing to choose Singapore partly so he can know what startup life and the city are like instead of staying with the known routine.
rationales.achievement: He describes a pattern of 'sampling everything, finishing nothing,' which he himself sees as undermining sustained accomplishment.
rationales.security: He knows that if he leaves and it fails, he 'can't just come back and pick up where I left off,' yet remains pulled toward the risky option.
rationales.conformity: He contrasts himself with relatives and friends who have 'figured out how to commit,' showing active tension with the stable life script around him.
rationales.benevolence: He turns down Aditya's dinner invite, admits 'I've been saying I'm busy a lot lately,' and calls himself 'a terrible friend and a terrible son.'

rationales.self_direction: He makes the decisive personal choice to 'accept the offer' and send the email rather than remain in the PhD by default.
rationales.stimulation: Accepting a move to Singapore in January clearly embraces a new environment and major change.
rationales.security: Leaving a doctoral program for an overseas startup is a direct move away from a more predictable path.
rationales.conformity: By accepting, he commits to a choice he has repeatedly framed as something others in his program and family may not approve of.

rationales.self_direction: He follows through with telling Sharma and his parents and keeps moving toward the path he chose rather than retreating.
rationales.stimulation: He describes the 'fizzy feeling' of 'something NEW coming' and sees even the worst case as a year in Singapore learning new things.
rationales.security: He knows this feeling has preceded past upheavals and still proceeds despite fearing he may get 'stuck again' and need 'the next exit.'

rationales.self_direction: He accepts being 'the one who's walking away' from the PhD instead of staying committed to an old promise from undergrad.
rationales.security: Packing to leave the program for a new country reflects a move away from the more stable path his earlier self imagined when he wrote 'I will definitely finish my PhD.'

rationales.self_direction: He says 'at some point you just have to move' and insists he needs to make 'A choice' rather than remain trapped by others' questions.
rationales.stimulation: He embraces Singapore because 'at least it'll be something different,' explicitly valuing change over the current path.
rationales.security: He rejects the PhD as 'safety' and chooses an uncertain future he cannot verify in advance.
rationales.conformity: He persists despite being repeatedly asked if he is 'sure,' after already questioning whether he is 'disappointing everyone.'

rationales.self_direction: She deliberately 'turned my phone off' and protected time where 'nobody needed anything from me,' asserting control over her day.
rationales.hedonism: She cooks shakshuka, drinks coffee, reads slowly, and says 'I needed it. My body needed it,' clearly prioritizing restorative pleasure and comfort.
Even with wine and music, she keeps calculating work and finally opens the laptop, saying she felt relieved to stop 'pretending I was relaxing.'
rationales.achievement: She returns to work for another two hours after already leaving early, showing strong pull toward productivity over rest.
rationales.benevolence: She justifies reopening the laptop because 'someone on my team might be stuck,' showing concern for close coworkers' needs.

rationales.hedonism: After a ten-hour day she wants to 'run a bath' but instead opens the laptop because she cannot even tell herself she 'deserved an hour off.'
rationales.achievement: She sacrifices recovery time to prep for tomorrow's review, prioritizing performance after an already exhausting workday.

rationales.self_direction: She chooses to leave the laptop behind and sit quietly at the coffee shop, acting on what she wants instead of defaulting to work.
rationales.hedonism: She lingers over a good cortado in 'low and gold' light and describes the pause as 'genuinely nice,' embracing a small pleasure for its own sake.
She goes to a restaurant she'd wanted to try, but spends 'half the meal thinking about the quarterly review deck' and leaves feeling absent from an enjoyable experience.
rationales.achievement: Work obligations dominate even during dinner with a friend, showing productivity demands overriding leisure.

rationales.security: She carefully manages each conference so there will be 'No difficult calls tomorrow,' prioritizing predictability and avoiding conflict escalation.
rationales.conformity: She performs the 'careful dance' of saying 'all the right things' and keeping the proper expression all day to meet institutional and social expectations.
rationales.benevolence: She chooses tiring diplomacy because she'd 'rather be tired than cause that kind of mess,' showing care for families and colleagues' relationships.

rationales.security: She follows protocol 'exactly' and emphasizes that looking away would mean acting as if 'rules don't matter,' prioritizing order and institutional safety.
rationales.conformity: She acts 'by the book' despite emotional discomfort and repeatedly frames the report as what was 'expected' and proper.
rationales.tradition: She leans on her mother's example that 'A teacher who ignores cheating isn't a teacher,' grounding the decision in inherited professional norms.
rationales.benevolence: She knows reporting Jia-Hui cost the student's trust and turns herself into 'the teacher who got her in trouble' rather than the supportive figure she wants to be.

rationales.achievement: She tells her mother she is 'focused on work right now' and frames exam period as too exhausting to think about anything else, prioritizing task completion.

rationales.self_direction: She admits 'most things I do are because someone is expecting me to do them' and that she has 'started to forget what I want when no one's watching.'
rationales.hedonism: She gives up a ceramics class she wants and settles for 'watching YouTube videos about ceramics instead,' denying herself a pleasurable activity.
rationales.conformity: She is the 'voice of the department' because she 'doesn't argue in meetings' and keeps smoothing interactions with polite restraint.
rationales.tradition: She lets family routine govern her choice, saying 'That's not how my family works. We show up for each other. That's the deal.'
rationales.benevolence: She does not sign up because it conflicts with her parents' usual visiting time and she prioritizes showing up for family over her own hobby.

rationales.self_direction: She keeps the registration form untouched and tells herself 'There's nothing wrong with my life the way it is,' suppressing the personal desire she keeps returning to.
rationales.hedonism: She again postpones the ceramics class she wants, letting worries about cost, grading, and family discussion override enjoyment.
rationales.security: She emphasizes having a 'stable job,' 'parents who care,' and 'a routine that works,' showing attachment to stability over experimentation.
rationales.conformity: At the dinner she behaves exactly as expected, smiling politely and noting the evening was 'exactly what it was supposed to be.'
rationales.benevolence: She centers 'Sunday dinners are important. Family is important,' prioritizing family obligations over her own leisure.
